_notes: v3 (2023/2024 merge): CI-G1 flips from unfunded to funded (EECBG grant confirmed by the 2024 Progress Report; see funding.json > eecbg-2024 > fundedActivities), while remaining dataGap:true for the 2025 report — the first 'funded but still gapped' case in this dataset, ending what had been a clean 100%-of-funded-goals-reported pattern. Computed over departments.json's 21 department goals only — the white-street-landfill entry has no 'goals' array (externalOnly) and is excluded by construction. Regenerate this file if departments.json's fundingLink/dataGap fields change.
unitOfAnalysis: department goal (21 total across 12 department/energy-source groupings)

crossTabCollapsed._notes: Same shape as prior versions, for continuity — 'funded' now = internal + external.

verdict.text: Adding the 2023/2024 reports surfaced a fifth funded goal — CI-G1 (Community Incentives), funded by the same December 2024 EECBG grant that also funds Rec 3/5/8 — that was NOT reported on in the 2025 Progress Report, ending what had been a clean 100%-of-funded-goals-reported pattern. The revised finding: 80% of funded goals were reported (4 of 5) vs. 12.5% of unfunded goals (2 of 16) — funding is still by far the strongest predictor of reporting, just not a perfect one anymore. CI-G1 is also a 'wentSilent' case (see departments.json): Initiated in both the 2023 and 2024 reports, then absent from 2025 entirely, despite real grant money behind it. A funded goal going silent is a stronger accountability signal than an unfunded one going silent, and is worth its own visual treatment. Separately, adding the externally-verified GTA funding (TR-D-G1) still moves one goal from 'unfunded-but-reported' to 'funded-and-reported': the GTA bus fleet is real, well-documented, and financially substantial ($22.4M+ across five federal/utility/settlement grants since 2016), and the Transit Director has publicly credited the SEP for the largest of these grants — yet none of it appears in either SEP source document, meaning the Progress Reports understate the SEP's demonstrated funding impact, not just its goal-tracking completeness.

citation.publisher: Gillig / City of Greensboro Transit Department
citation.title: Transit Department Awarded $22.4 Million for Battery-Electric Buses, Hybrid Buses and Vehicle Chargers

byDepartmentGrouping.note: Updated in the 2023/2024 merge: CI-G1 is now funded (EECBG) but remains gapped; CI-G2 remains unfunded and gapped. Both goals in this grouping are unreported for 2025, despite one being funded.

notablePattern: Natural gas goals (Coliseum, Parks & Rec, Fire — 4 goals total) remain a clean 100% funding/reporting blackout, and are explicitly flagged `deprioritizedInSource` in departments.json — the 2022 SEP itself deprioritized natural gas, so this gap is a documented choice made visible, not a surprising omission, and should read differently in the UI than the other gapped goals. Separately, three goals (TR-GAS-G1, FO-G1, CI-G1) now carry a `wentSilent` flag: confirmed Initiated activity in both the 2023 and 2024 reports, followed by zero mention in 2025. CI-G1 is the most notable of the three since it's also funded — a goal with real grant money behind it that still dropped out of the newest report.

goalLevelDetail.note: Has a relatedExternalFinding (White Street Landfill solar) — not counted as funded since it's not this goal's own project, but flagged for UI cross-reference.
Funded AND wentSilent — the notable exception to the 'funding predicts reporting' pattern.
